scene.description: Engine 181 is pumping a standpipe operation at a 5-story apartment building. Crews are operating from the 5th-floor standpipe outlet, which is 40 feet above pump level using the floor-height rule (1st floor = 0 feet, each floor above adds 10 feet). The crew has 150’ of 2½” hose and a 1⅛” smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM at 50 PSI. Add 25 PSI for standpipe/FDC system loss. What pump pressure should the driver/operator set?

nozzles: 1⅛” smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM at 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: 5th floor outlet is 40 feet above pump level, +20 PSI

problems.title: Advanced Standpipe Floor 5
problems.question: Engine 181 is pumping a standpipe operation at a 5-story apartment building. Crews are operating from the 5th-floor standpipe outlet, which is 40 feet above pump level using the floor-height rule: 1st floor = 0 feet, 2nd floor = 10 feet, 3rd floor = 20 feet, 4th floor = 30 feet, 5th floor = 40 feet. The crew has 150’ of 2½” hose and a 1⅛” smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM at 50 PSI. Add 25 PSI for standpipe/FDC system loss. What pump pressure should the driver/operator set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds
Floor-height rule: 1st floor = 0 feet, each floor above adds 10 feet
Elevation = 40 feet × 0.5 PSI per foot = 20 PSI
problems.instructorExplanation: This scenario uses the corrected floor-height rule: the 1st floor is pump level/0 feet, and each floor above adds 10 feet. A 5th-floor outlet is therefore 40 feet above pump level, not 50 feet. Elevation is 40 × 0.5 = 20 PSI. Add hose friction loss, nozzle pressure, elevation, and standpipe/FDC system loss, then round the final pump pressure to 115 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes include treating the 5th floor as 5 full floors above the pump instead of 4 floor rises, using 25 PSI elevation instead of 20 PSI, forgetting the 25 PSI standpipe/FDC loss, using the wrong hose coefficient, or rounding before the final pump pressure calculation.
